questions.context:
In a class of 30 students, 18 study Biology and 20 study Chemistry.
All students study at least one of these subjects.

parts.label: a
parts.questionText: Find the number of students who study both Biology and Chemistry.
workingSteps: B + C − Both = Total (since all study at least one)

parts.label: b
parts.questionText:
A student is chosen at random.
Given that this student studies Biology, find the probability that they also study Chemistry.
workingSteps: P(C | B) = number studying both / number studying Biology

parts.label: c
parts.questionText:
Two students are chosen at random from those who study Biology.
Find the probability that both also study Chemistry.

markScheme.desc: 8 students study both out of 18 Biology students

questions.context:
A medical test for a disease is not perfect.
- If a person has the disease, the test is positive with probability 0.95.
- If a person does not have the disease, the test is positive with probability 0.02.
- 1% of the population has the disease.

parts.label: a
parts.questionText:
A person is selected at random and tested.
Complete the tree diagram and find the probability that the test is positive.

markScheme.desc: Tree: Disease (0.01) → Pos (0.95), Neg (0.05); No disease (0.99) → Pos (0.02), Neg (0.98)

parts.label: b
parts.questionText:
Given that the test is positive, find the probability that the person actually has the disease.
Give your answer correct to 3 significant figures.

markScheme.desc: This means about 2/3 of positive tests are false positives

workingSteps: Only about 32% of positive results are true positives

questions.questionText:
A drawer contains 6 black socks and 4 white socks.
Three socks are taken out at random without replacement.
Find the probability that all three socks are the same colour.
questions.explanation: Add P(all black) and P(all white). Use without-replacement probabilities.
